data.description:
RNS Number : 9679H Mears Group PLC 08 May 2025  

Mears Group PLC

("Mears" or "the Group" or "the Company")

Exercise of options and PDMR dealing

Mears Group PLC announces that on 6 May 2025, Andrew Smith, Chief Financial Officer and David Miles, Operations Director, both PDMRs, exercised share options over 484,671 ordinary shares of 1 pence each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares") in connection with the Company's Long Term Incentive Plan and Deferred Share Bonus Plan as detailed below.

Subsequent to the exercise of the share options, on 8 May 2025, Andrew Smith has sold 108,411 Ordinary Shares and David Miles has sold 162,348 Ordinary Shares at an average price of 393 pence per Ordinary Share in order to satisfy tax liabilities resulting from the exercise of the above-mentioned options.

About Mears

Mears is a leading provider of services to the Housing sector, providing a range of services to individuals within their homes. We manage and maintain around 450,000 homes across the UK and work predominantly with Central Government and Local Government, typically through long-term contracts. We equally consider the residents of the homes that we manage and maintain to be our customers, and we take pride in the high levels of customer satisfaction that we achieve.

Mears currently employs over 5,000 people and provides services in every region of the UK. In partnership with our Housing clients, we provide property management and maintenance services. Mears has extended its activities to provide broader housing solutions to solve the challenge posed by the lack of affordable housing and to provide accommodation and support for the most vulnerable.

We focus on long-term outcomes for people rather than short-term solutions and invest in innovations that have a positive impact on people's quality of life and on their communities' social, economic, and environmental wellbeing. Our innovative approaches and market leading positions are intended to create value for our customers and the people they serve while also driving sustainable financial returns for our providers of capital, especially our shareholders.

The information set out below is provided in accordance with the requirements of Article 19(3) of the EU Market Abuse Regulation No 596/2014 (as it forms part of domestic law by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018).

08 May 2025

Drax Group plc

Transaction in own shares

Drax Group plc ("Drax Group") announces that, in accordance with the terms of its share buyback programme (the "Programme") announced on 7 August 2024, it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of 11 16/29 pence each ("Ordinary Shares") through RBC Europe Limited.

08 May 2025

Following the above transaction, Drax Group will have 429,593,373 Ordinary Shares in issue (including treasury shares). The total number of Ordinary Shares held in treasury is 73,783,133.

The total number of voting rights in Drax Group, excluding treasury shares as at 08 May 2025 is 355,810,240. This figure may be used by shareholders (and others with notification obligations) as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, Drax Group under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

Since the announcement of the Programme on 7 August 2024, Drax Group has purchased 33,810,141 Ordinary Shares in aggregate for treasury.

data.title: Lendistry Continues to Help Small Businesses Grow Across the Country With Investment From Starbucks Coffee Company
data.description:
This National Small Business Week, the coffee company's Community Resilience Fund demonstrates the importance of large businesses supporting economic opportunities for small businesses

LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA / ACCESS Newswire / May 8, 2025 / Lendistry, a nationwide small business lender and Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI), announces today that it has received an investment from Starbucks Coffee Company through its Community Resilience Fund. Founded in 2021, the company committed to invest $100 million to support small business growth and community development projects with limited access to capital-a mission it shares with Lendistry.

"We appreciate Starbucks Coffee Company for using their stature to help small businesses grow," says Lendistry CEO, Everett K. Sands. "By investing in the capital needs of small businesses, Starbucks is supporting the creation of jobs and wealth for local communities - enabling neighborhoods across the country to be vibrant places to live and work."

"As the community coffeehouse, we see firsthand how small businesses make our communities special," said Kelly Goodejohn, chief social impact officer at Starbucks. "When local entrepreneurs have what they need to succeed, it creates a ripple effect that makes our neighborhoods even stronger."

This investment from Starbucks fuels Lendistry's impact, and Lendistry puts those funds into action as smaller loans to businesses across the country, creating a capital continuum that benefits all.

About Lendistry

Lendistry (lendistry.com) is a tech-enabled small businesses lender, grant administrator for private and public agencies, and a trusted resource for undercapitalized entrepreneurs. Founded in 2015, Lendistry has used technology and community partnerships to deploy over $10 billion in its first ten years. Lendistry was recently named the winner of the LA Area Chamber of Commerce Corporate Leadership Award. Lendistry has both Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) and Community Development Entity (CDE) certifications, is an SBA Preferred Lender and is now the #2 non-bank SBA 7(a) lender in the country. In collaboration with The Center by Lendistry, a nonprofit business education organization, Lendistry helps business owners achieve their goals and prepare to scale.

Contact Information

Kate Kearns

Sr. Communications Manager

communications@lendistry.com

SOURCE: Lendistry

data.title: Groupon Inc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Groupon Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 18 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of -33 cents. The mean expectation of four analysts for the quarter was for a loss of 11 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from -15 cents to -2 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 4.8% to $117.19 million from a year ago; analysts expected $115.50 million.

  • Groupon Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 17 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $7.18 million.

  • Groupon Inc shares had fallen by 9.5% this quarter and gained 39.8% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 23.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 3 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the discount stores peer group is "hold."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Groupon Inc is $20.50

data.title: UK's Rolls-Royce and Melrose rise on 'breakthrough' trade deal with US
data.description:
** Shares of UK's Rolls-Royce up 3.7% at 794p; aerospace components supplier Melrose also up 5.2% at 470.70p

** Melrose top pct gainer and RR among top pct gainers on on FTSE 100 Index CURRENCYCOM:UK100

** U.S. President Donald Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer reached a "breakthrough deal" on trade

** Deal maintains a 10% tariff on goods imported from the UK that has agreed to lower its tariff rate to 1.8% from 5.1% and give more access to U.S. goods

** Deal's details not disclosed completely, but seems favorable for UK's key exports such as cars and aerospace engines, prompting rise in stock prices for Rolls Royce and Melrose, says Lindsay James, investment strategist at Quilter

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1241 ET - The limited details offered during a press conference at the Oval Office in which President Trump announced a new trade agreement with the UK has left some analysts underwhelmed. "Along with the push to get trade talks with China underway too this weekend, this rush to demonstrate progress on 'deals' reveals a rising desperation within the administration to rollback tariffs before they hit GDP growth and inflation," says Paul Ashworth of Capital Economics in a note following the announcement. Ashworth adds that "even the broad details still appear to be vague," although comments from Trump suggest that a 10% baseline tariff looks to remain in place. Focus is now turning to this weekend's meeting between U.S. and Chinese officials in Switzerland. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1235 ET - The U.S.-UK trade deal is expected to be a boon for U.S. beef demand. In a statement after President Trump's announcement, the National Cattlemen's Beef Association called the U.K. "an ideal partner for trade." The NCBA says that the U.K. became a big opportunity for American beef suppliers after the country left the EU, as U.K. consumers are big meat consumers. In the first two months of 2025 the U.K. imported roughly 47,000 metric tons of beef — with over 30,000 tons coming from Ireland, according to data from U.K.'s Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board. In that timeframe, the U.K. has only committed to purchase just over 1,000 tons of U.S. beef, according to USDA data. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1228 ET - News of a trade agreement between the U.K. and U.S. is welcome given the uncertainty about tariffs and their economic impact hovering over markets, Joaquin Thul, economist at EFG Asset Management writes in a note. However, its impact will be limited in the short term, he says. The deal isn't comprehensive but acts as a statement of intent to work towards improving trade between both economies. Tariffs on U.K. steel and aluminum products are expected to be reduced while allowing preferential access of U.S. beef, ethanol and other agricultural products into the U.K. market, he says. "Overall, news of trade agreements between U.K. and U.S. are welcome in the global context of trade uncertainty," Thul says. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

1223 ET - Sterling rises to a one-month high against the euro after the announcement of a trade deal between the U.S. and U.K., which analysts say is welcome, albeit limited in scope. The deal includes a reduction in tariffs on exports of autos as well as steel and aluminum tariffs. "This deal will be met with a huge sigh of relief by many British businesses," says Shevaun Haviland, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce, in a note. However, analysts say the deal could offer more benefits to the U.S. than the U.K. and the pound loses ground against the dollar. The euro falls 0.5% to a low of 0.8458, according to FactSet. Sterling loses 0.2% to $1.3268 against the dollar, having traded higher earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1218 ET - Carvana CEO Ernie Garcia thinks an argument can be made for and against the company in a higher tariff world. Analysts have largely said higher prices for new vehicles by way of tariffs may lead to higher interest in used cars. "To the extent new car prices go up, I think generally that would pull up used car prices but less. I think that would likely drive a substitution into used," said Garcia in an interview with CNBC's "Squawk Box. "Reasonable arguments could be made for us that it's a positive, I think reasonable arguments could be made that car prices go up and that's a negative." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1202 ET - The newly announced U.K.-U.S. trade deal won't change economic forecasts, says Oxford Economics deputy chief economist Michael Pearce. The deal offers limited relief from autos, steel and aluminum tariffs, while the broader 10% tariff remains in place, he says. While exemptions will nibble away at the effective tariff rate, the average U.S. tariff is set to remain in double digits, delivering a big hit to incomes in the U.S. that will cause growth to slow sharply in the second half of 2025, he says in a note. The U.K. has made some commitments to open agricultural markets as well as chemicals and machinery, though further details will be key. The bigger question remains the future of tariffs on China, Pearce says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Investors are better off taking an active approach to investing in U.K. stocks given the varying impact of global policy uncertainty, says Vivek Paul, U.K. chief investment strategist at BlackRock Investment Institute, in a note. The U.K. economy is likely to suffer minimal direct impact from the U.S. tariffs. Most of the impact will be via tariff uncertainty and a potential global slowdown reducing demand for U.K. goods and services. "The U.K. already benefits from some exclusions to the tariffs announced on 2 April - for example on chemicals, which make up a sizable share of U.K. exports to the U.S.," he says. President Trump announced a trade agreement with the U.K. Thursday, which U.K. officials say focuses on reducing tariffs in specific sectors. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1147 ET - Yields on U.K. 2-year government bonds rise to a 2-week high of 3.923% after the Bank of England earlier signalled that future interest-rate cuts would be gradual and after the announcement of a U.S.-U.K. trade deal. The BOE cut interest rates earlier but this was already expected and gilt investors reacted to the fact that two policymakers voted to keep rates on hold, which was unexpected, says AJ Bell's Laith Khalaf in a note. The trade agreement with the U.S., although it isn't comprehensive, could help U.K. economic growth and further reduce the possibility of rapid interest-rate cuts. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1130 ET - New homes are offering an affordability edge in the current challenging housing market, according to Realtor.com. In 1Q, the median list price for newly built homes fell slightly year-over-year to $448,393, reducing the price gap with existing homes to its lowest 1Q level in five years. This, combined with builders building smaller homes, and lower mortgage rates for new home buyers, is making newly built homes a more accessible pathway to homeownership. The U.S. is short about four million homes, and new construction is filling the affordability gap left by a tight existing market, Realtor.com says. Builders are delivering smaller homes at lower prices and often offering financial incentives that make monthly payments more manageable. But tariffs could limit this progress and create new cost pressures in the months ahead. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1127 ET - Gold futures slide in volatile trading on a stronger U.S. dollar and a recovery in risk sentiment. Futures are down 1% at $3,357.80 a troy ounce. The dollar has found some solid footing, logging its third straight week of modest gains, while U.S. indexes and bitcoin have rallied, StoneX's Fawad Razaqzada says in a note. This renewed appetite for risk has dragged on gold's safe-haven appeal. Optimism around trade--with U.S. negotiations with China on the table for the weekend and a deal with the U.K. announced--have driven the rally, Razaqzada says. That said, while the market is pricing in some optimism for a China deal, the negotiations could be long and drawn-out, limiting the upside potential for risk assets and gold's slide, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1120 ET - President Trump says that a trade deal with the U.K. will open the country up to 'billions' in U.S. exports. Speaking from the White House, the President notes that agricultural exports will be a big part of that. No specifics were offered, but for U.S. agriculture demand from the U.K. has been minimal. Corn export sales were roughly 66,000 metric tons in the 2023/24 marketing year, according to USDA data — with soybean sales at 120,389 tons and wheat sales at 10,450 tons. These figures are a very small fraction of overall U.S. agricultural export sales. Specifics on the deal are expected in upcoming weeks, Trump told media at the White House. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Details on a deal between the U.S. and U.K. are coming Thursday after President Trump made an earlier announcement, much is unclear for the U.K. side, J.P. Morgan's Allan Monks says in a research note. The analyst notes that it remains to be seen whether the U.K. can arrange an exemption from the baseline 10% tariff following subsequent negotiations. But he adds that with the U.K. turning to trade discussions with the EU, subsequent progress on key outstanding issues with the U.S. could become harder. "With the UK having broadly balanced goods trade with the US, a reasonably good political relationship, no real threat of retaliation from Westminster and extensive bilateral negotiations having taken place, it is not clear where the UK can go from here," says Monks. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1241 ET - The limited details offered during a press conference at the Oval Office in which President Trump announced a new trade agreement with the UK has left some analysts underwhelmed. "Along with the push to get trade talks with China underway too this weekend, this rush to demonstrate progress on 'deals' reveals a rising desperation within the administration to rollback tariffs before they hit GDP growth and inflation," says Paul Ashworth of Capital Economics in a note following the announcement. Ashworth adds that "even the broad details still appear to be vague," although comments from Trump suggest that a 10% baseline tariff looks to remain in place. Focus is now turning to this weekend's meeting between U.S. and Chinese officials in Switzerland. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1228 ET - News of a trade agreement between the U.K. and U.S. is welcome given the uncertainty about tariffs and their economic impact hovering over markets, Joaquin Thul, economist at EFG Asset Management writes in a note. However, its impact will be limited in the short term, he says. The deal isn't comprehensive but acts as a statement of intent to work towards improving trade between both economies. Tariffs on U.K. steel and aluminum products are expected to be reduced while allowing preferential access of U.S. beef, ethanol and other agricultural products into the U.K. market, he says. "Overall, news of trade agreements between U.K. and U.S. are welcome in the global context of trade uncertainty," Thul says. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

1223 ET - Sterling rises to a one-month high against the euro after the announcement of a trade deal between the U.S. and U.K., which analysts say is welcome, albeit limited in scope. The deal includes a reduction in tariffs on exports of autos as well as steel and aluminum tariffs. "This deal will be met with a huge sigh of relief by many British businesses," says Shevaun Haviland, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce, in a note. However, analysts say the deal could offer more benefits to the U.S. than the U.K. and the pound loses ground against the dollar. The euro falls 0.5% to a low of 0.8458, according to FactSet. Sterling loses 0.2% to $1.3268 against the dollar, having traded higher earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1212 ET - The latest batch of economic data out Thursday reiterate a familiar tale: Despite deep economic uncertainty, there's no serious sign of deterioration in the records of what has actually happened in the economy. Jobless claims retreated to a moderate 228,000 last week after an aberrant uptick the week before. Productivity fell by 0.8% in the first quarter, but that measurement sprung from a likely quirky, trade-distorted set of stats that may well get revised, Don Rissmiller of research firm Strategas writes to clients. "Bottom line: the 'hard' economic data still do not indicate a U.S. economy in distress," he writes. Consumers seem to be hanging in there despite the anxiety they report in surveys, but Rissmiller adds a note of caution: "These supports cannot last indefinitely." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1204 ET - The pickup in Mexico's inflation to 3.93% in April is seen unlikely to keep the Bank of Mexico from cutting its benchmark interest rate by a half percentage point to 8.5% next week, although rising pressure on core nonfood goods items is attracting attention. "This will be something to monitor amid renewed pressures on automobile prices and trade uncertainty, but we think well-behaved FX in recent weeks should help to moderate it," analysts at Barclays say in a note. Beyond seasonal noise such as the Easter holiday in April, core services aren't showing evidence of demand pressures, they add. "We still think inflation will head south in the summer and Banxico will continue to deliver 50bp cuts." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1202 ET - The newly announced U.K.-U.S. trade deal won't change economic forecasts, says Oxford Economics deputy chief economist Michael Pearce. The deal offers limited relief from autos, steel and aluminum tariffs, while the broader 10% tariff remains in place, he says. While exemptions will nibble away at the effective tariff rate, the average U.S. tariff is set to remain in double digits, delivering a big hit to incomes in the U.S. that will cause growth to slow sharply in the second half of 2025, he says in a note. The U.K. has made some commitments to open agricultural markets as well as chemicals and machinery, though further details will be key. The bigger question remains the future of tariffs on China, Pearce says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1150 ET - U.K. long-dated gilts look more appealing than their U.S. peers as the U.K.'s term premium better reflects analysts expectations of where interest rates will settle in the long-term, says Vivek Paul, U.K. chief investment strategist at BlackRock Investment Institute, in a note. The term premium is the extra compensation investors demand for holding long-dated bonds over shorter-dated bonds. This is expected to rise globally but the U.K. term premium looks favorable relative to the U.S. term premium, Paul says. U.K. 30-year gilt yields last trade at 5.310%. The U.S. 30-year Treasury yield is much lower, last trading at 4.805%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Investors are better off taking an active approach to investing in U.K. stocks given the varying impact of global policy uncertainty, says Vivek Paul, U.K. chief investment strategist at BlackRock Investment Institute, in a note. The U.K. economy is likely to suffer minimal direct impact from the U.S. tariffs. Most of the impact will be via tariff uncertainty and a potential global slowdown reducing demand for U.K. goods and services. "The U.K. already benefits from some exclusions to the tariffs announced on 2 April - for example on chemicals, which make up a sizable share of U.K. exports to the U.S.," he says. President Trump announced a trade agreement with the U.K. Thursday, which U.K. officials say focuses on reducing tariffs in specific sectors. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1147 ET - Yields on U.K. 2-year government bonds rise to a 2-week high of 3.923% after the Bank of England earlier signalled that future interest-rate cuts would be gradual and after the announcement of a U.S.-U.K. trade deal. The BOE cut interest rates earlier but this was already expected and gilt investors reacted to the fact that two policymakers voted to keep rates on hold, which was unexpected, says AJ Bell's Laith Khalaf in a note. The trade agreement with the U.S., although it isn't comprehensive, could help U.K. economic growth and further reduce the possibility of rapid interest-rate cuts. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1130 ET - New homes are offering an affordability edge in the current challenging housing market, according to Realtor.com. In 1Q, the median list price for newly built homes fell slightly year-over-year to $448,393, reducing the price gap with existing homes to its lowest 1Q level in five years. This, combined with builders building smaller homes, and lower mortgage rates for new home buyers, is making newly built homes a more accessible pathway to homeownership. The U.S. is short about four million homes, and new construction is filling the affordability gap left by a tight existing market, Realtor.com says. Builders are delivering smaller homes at lower prices and often offering financial incentives that make monthly payments more manageable. But tariffs could limit this progress and create new cost pressures in the months ahead. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1120 ET - President Trump says that a trade deal with the U.K. will open the country up to 'billions' in U.S. exports. Speaking from the White House, the President notes that agricultural exports will be a big part of that. No specifics were offered, but for U.S. agriculture demand from the U.K. has been minimal. Corn export sales were roughly 66,000 metric tons in the 2023/24 marketing year, according to USDA data — with soybean sales at 120,389 tons and wheat sales at 10,450 tons. These figures are a very small fraction of overall U.S. agricultural export sales. Specifics on the deal are expected in upcoming weeks, Trump told media at the White House. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Details on a deal between the U.S. and U.K. are coming Thursday after President Trump made an earlier announcement, much is unclear for the U.K. side, J.P. Morgan's Allan Monks says in a research note. The analyst notes that it remains to be seen whether the U.K. can arrange an exemption from the baseline 10% tariff following subsequent negotiations. But he adds that with the U.K. turning to trade discussions with the EU, subsequent progress on key outstanding issues with the U.S. could become harder. "With the UK having broadly balanced goods trade with the US, a reasonably good political relationship, no real threat of retaliation from Westminster and extensive bilateral negotiations having taken place, it is not clear where the UK can go from here," says Monks. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: US FDA approves Verastem's cancer therapy
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Verastem's combination therapy for patients with a type of ovarian cancer who have received prior treatment, the regulator said on Thursday.

The FDA approved the combination under the brand name, Avmapki Fakzynja Co-pack, for adult patients with a mutated form of the gene known as KRAS.

KRAS-mutated recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer is a type of cancer that arises after initial treatment for the disease caused by a mutation in the KRAS gene.

Verastem did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

Share of the company were halted in mid-day trading.

data.title: U.S.-U.K. Trade Deal Seen as a Win for U.S. Beef — Market Talk
data.description: The U.S.-UK trade deal is expected to be a boon for U.S. beef demand. In a statement after President Trump's announcement, the National Cattlemen's Beef Association called the U.K. "an ideal partner for trade." The NCBA says that the U.K. became a big opportunity for American beef suppliers after the country left the EU, as U.K. consumers are big meat consumers. In the first two months of 2025 the U.K. imported roughly 47,000 metric tons of beef — with over 30,000 tons coming from Ireland, according to data from U.K.'s Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board. In that timeframe, the U.K. has only committed to purchase just over 1,000 tons of U.S. beef, according to USDA data. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: goeasy Target Cut to $235 at National Bank After Q1
data.description:
National Bank has cut its target on goeasy to $235 (from $240) after the company reported first-quarter adjusted EPS of $3.53, down 8%, and below the street $4.20 and NBF $4.06.

National Bank analyst Jaeme Gloyn described the first quarter as "a noisy quarter." Despite the EPS miss, the company delivered a solid quarter of growth and sequential charge-off and delinquency improvement that should calm investor concerns regarding the credit picture, he added.

Maintain Outperform.

data.title: Enel Backs Full-Year Guidance as Earnings Rise
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Enel confirmed its full-year guidance after its first-quarter earnings were boosted by its integrated business in Iberia and the Americas.

The Rome-based energy company on Thursday reported first-quarter net profit of 2.00 billion euros (2.27 billion). This compares with 1.83 billion euros in the same period a year prior. On an adjusted basis, net profit--or net ordinary income--rose 1.5% to 2.00 billion euros.

Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization increased 5.3% to 5.97 billion euros. Adjusted for one-offs, Ebitda--or ordinary Ebitda--rose 1.7% to 5.97 billion euros.

Revenue rose nearly 14% to 22.07 billion euros on higher quantities of electricity produced and sales of commodities on the wholesale market, the company said.

The company backed its full-year guidance. It expects ordinary Ebitda between 22.9 billion and 23.1 billion euros, and net ordinary income between 6.7 billion and 6.9 billion euros.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Wall St rises after US-Britain trade deal boosts market confidence
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes trade at over one-month highs
  • US-UK trade deal reduces tariffs on US goods
  • Boeing gains after UK to buy planes worth $10 bln
  • Arm slides after bleak Q1 revenue forecast
  • Krispy Kreme sinks after withdrawing FY forecasts

Wall Street's main indexes climbed more than 1% each on Thursday as investors assessed a new trade agreement between the United States and Britain, the first such trade deal the U.S. has signed after President Donald Trump paused tariffs last month.

Britain agreed to lower its tariffs to 1.8% from 5.1% and provide greater access to U.S. goods as part of the deal, while a 10% baseline tariff on goods imported from the UK into the U.S. remains in place.

Airline stocks jumped after the U.S.-UK agreement exempted plane parts made by Rolls-Royce from tariffs, with the S&P Passenger Airlines Index (.SPLRCALI) up 5.3%.

U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said the UK would buy $10 billion of Boeing aircraft , sending the planemaker's shares up 3.5%.

Trump said he expects substantive negotiations between the U.S. and Beijing on the trade front this weekend. He also said tariffs on Chinese imports could not get higher than 145%.

"This is what the market has been looking for... some sort of a breakthrough. This does not put to rest some of the uncertainties regarding the trade war effects, but it's a start," said Peter Cardillo, chief market economist at Spartan Capital Securities.

At 11:56 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 516.11 points, or 1.26%, to 41,630.08, the S&P 500 gained 68.03 points, or 1.21%, to 5,699.31, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 280.94 points, or 1.58%, to 18,019.10.

Energy stocks led gains among the S&P 500's 11 sectors with a 2.4% rise, tracking a jump in crude prices.

The domestically focused Russell 2000 small-cap index added 2% and touched a more than one-month high. Most megacap and growth stocks were higher, with Tesla leading gains with a 4.7% rise.

Semiconductor stocks <.SOX> inched 1.9% higher, building on the previous session's gains after a spokesperson said the Trump administration was planning to rescind and modify a rule that curbed the export of sophisticated artificial-intelligence chips.

The U.S. Federal Reserve held interest rates steady on Wednesday and flagged heightened risks of inflation and unemployment, further clouding the economic outlook for the world's largest economy.

Traders now see a rate cut only by September and are pricing in a total of 73 basis points of lowering by 2025-end, according to data compiled by LSEG.

On the data front, jobless claims fell more than expected last week, suggesting the labor market continued to chug along.

Among others, U.S.-listed shares of Arm plunged 4.8% after the chipmaker forecast first-quarter revenue and profit below Wall Street estimates.

Tapestry added 4.1% after the luxury group raised its annual forecasts, while Fortinet slumped 8.2% after the cybersecurity firm's second-quarter revenue forecast came in below estimates.

Warner Bros. Discovery gained 4.9% after a report said the company was headed for a split. The streaming firm missed its quarterly revenue estimates.

Krispy Kreme's shares sank more than 24% after the restaurant chain became the latest to withdraw its full-year forecast.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 3.04-to-1 ratio on the NYSE, and by a 2.39-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted 17 new 52-week highs and four new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 43 new highs and 79 new lows.

data.title: UBS Comments on The Swiss Franc
data.description:
The Swiss franc (CHF) should remain broadly stable, with only limited further gains as trade tensions ease, UBS said in its base case scenario.

UBS noted it expects the Swiss central bank (SNB) to cut its policy rate to 0% in June and and intervene sporadically in the foreign exchange market to reduce the franc's appreciation pressures.

In this scenario, the short-term deviation of the Swiss franc's real effective exchange rate from its long-term

trend is expected to be limited, so warranting a

measured reaction by the SNB, in the bank's view.

USDCHF and EURCHF are likely to trade sideways at

0.80-0.85 and 0.93-0.96 over the next year, according to UBS.
